meta.title: HALO Procedures QIP — Improving Readiness for High Acuity Low Occurrence Procedures

checklist.problem_desc: Preliminary scoping (October 2025) identified a critical patient safety variance in the Emergency Department. While centralised equipment (e.g. the Central Line Trolley) was located in 91 seconds, dispersed High Acuity Low Occurrence (HALO) procedure equipment (e.g. Thoracotomy kit) took over 5 minutes (>300 seconds) to retrieve. A pilot Stopwatch Audit (N=3) confirmed this finding statistically. The infrastructure directly dictates retrieval speed, regardless of clinician seniority. This 'Time to Kit' delay creates a preventable safety gap during time-critical emergency interventions where every second directly impacts patient survival.
checklist.problem_context:
Emergency Department, West Midlands (WMEBEM). HALO procedures require immediate action but current equipment storage is fragmented across multiple sites — Resus cupboards, theatres, and various store areas — increasing cognitive friction and retrieval time during high-stress, time-critical scenarios.

GOVERNANCE & SAFETY STANDARDS (LOCSSIPs / NatSSIPs):
This project functions under the framework of NatSSIPs (National Safety Standards for Invasive Procedures). The HALO Trolley acts as the physical infrastructure to support Departmental LOCSSIPs (Local Safety Standards for Invasive Procedures). QRH guides serve as the immediate safety checklist required by LOCSSIPs. Trolley design and location vetted by the Infection Prevention Control Lead. Stock discrepancies reported via Datix to the Patient Safety Team.

PATIENT & PUBLIC INVOLVEMENT (PPI):
This project addresses the patient safety priority: 'Right Kit, Right Place, Right Time'. Delays in HALO scenarios (Traumatic Arrest, Perimortem Caesarean, Resuscitative Thoracotomy) are directly linked to patient mortality. Project governance has been reviewed by the Trust Patient Safety Group. The project also mitigates the psychological impact on staff ('second victim') by reducing cognitive load during high-stress crises.

VERSION HISTORY: v7.0 (Oct 2025) — Initial project; v8.0 (Feb 2026) — Stakeholder log, evidence base formalised; v9.0 (Apr 2026) — PDSA cycles reordered, trolley quote confirmed, tag system agreed, booklet distributed.
checklist.problem_evidence:
PILOT SCOPING DATA (Proof of Concept, N=3, October 2025):
• Control — Centralised stock (Central Line Trolley): 91 seconds ✓
• Test — Dispersed HALO stock (Thoracotomy kit): >300 seconds (>5 minutes) ✗
• Conclusion: Centralising equipment reduces retrieval time by >60%

FULL BASELINE DATASET (N=13, combined Pilot + Phase 1 stopwatch timings):
• Baseline Median 'Time to Kit': 245 seconds
• High variation across procedures and staff
• Target: ≤120 seconds (50% reduction from median)

PRE-INTERVENTION CONFIDENCE SURVEY (Staff Likert Scale):
• Staff reporting 'confident to assist/perform HALO procedures with guidance': 9%
• Target: ≥90%

HALO PROCEDURES REQUIRING TROLLEY (8 core procedures):
Resuscitative Hysterotomy, Resuscitative Thoracotomy, Escharotomy (Burns), Chest Drain (Open), Pericardiocentesis, Subclavian Central Line, Lateral Canthotomy, Severe Max-Fax Bleed
checklist.aim: By June 2026: (1) Increase the percentage of Emergency Department staff reporting they are 'confident to assist with or perform HALO procedures with guidance' from 9% to ≥90%; and (2) Reduce the mean 'Time to Kit' for 8 core HALO procedures by 50% (from a baseline median of 245 seconds to ≤120 seconds) in the Emergency Department.
checklist.aim_measure: Time to Kit (seconds) for 8 core HALO procedures; % staff confidence rating ≥ 'confident with guidance'
checklist.aim_baseline: Time to Kit: Median 245s (N=13); Staff Confidence: 9%
checklist.aim_target: Time to Kit: ≤120s (50% reduction); Staff Confidence: ≥90%
checklist.outcome_measure:
PRIMARY OUTCOME 1 — 'Time to Kit' (seconds):
Method: Stopwatch Audit (In-situ simulation). Timer starts at 'Scenario Declaration' (e.g. 'Traumatic Arrest') to capture cognitive load and mitigate Hawthorne effect. Goal: Reduce mean time by ≥50% from baseline median of 245s.

PRIMARY OUTCOME 2 — Procedural Knowledge Score (Pre/Post QRH deployment):
Method: Structured knowledge assessment administered before and after QRH deployment to all ED staff. Analysis: Wilcoxon signed-rank test to assess statistical significance of score changes. Goal: Demonstrate statistically significant improvement in procedural knowledge scores.
checklist.process_measure:
PROCESS MEASURE — Checklist Compliance %:
Method: Weekly audit of the 'Broken Tag Log'. Tag integrity checked twice daily at handover. Goal: >95% of broken tags have a corresponding staff signature and replacement within the same shift.
checklist.balance_measure:
BALANCING MEASURE — Stock Cost & Theft:
Method: Monthly procurement review with Stores Lead (Khan Zaman) and Charlotte Vineham (Ops Manager). Goal: <£100 loss per month.

SECONDARY BALANCING — Staff Burden:
Goal: Tag system and restocking protocol add <5 minutes per shift to nursing workload.
checklist.ethics:
ETHICS & REGISTRATION:
This project is classified as a Quality Improvement initiative in accordance with the NHS Health Research Authority (HRA) decision tool and does not constitute research. No patient-identifiable data is collected at any stage. All timing data is anonymised at source and linked only to staff role. Staff participation in the 'Stopwatch Audit' and knowledge assessments is voluntary. No formal ethical approval from a Research Ethics Committee (REC) is required.

The project has been registered with the Trust Clinical Audit & Effectiveness Department in accordance with local governance requirements.

NatSSIPs / LOCSSIPs COMPLIANCE:
Project design, trolley layout, and IPC compliance have been reviewed in accordance with NatSSIPs. QRH guides serve as the immediate safety checklist required by LOCSSIPs. Trolley location vetted by the Infection Prevention Control Lead for cleaning compliance. Discrepancies in stock will be reported via Datix to the Patient Safety Team.

SQUIRE 2.0 METHODOLOGY:
The project follows SQUIRE 2.0 reporting standards. Work as Done vs Work as Imagined addressed through qualitative questionnaire in baseline scoping phase.
checklist.lit_review:
RCEM QUALITY STANDARDS FOR MAJOR TRAUMA (2024):
• Emphasises the importance of immediately accessible procedural equipment for HALO emergency scenarios.
• Cited as the primary standard supporting centralisation of HALO equipment.

NATIONAL CONFIDENTIAL ENQUIRY INTO PATIENT OUTCOME AND DEATH (NCEPOD):
• Repeatedly highlights equipment retrieval delays as a contributory factor in suboptimal care during emergency procedures.
• Supports the 'Right Kit, Right Place, Right Time' patient safety framing of this project.

COGNITIVE OFFLOADING IN HIGH-RELIABILITY INDUSTRIES:
• Well-established in aviation, military medicine, and Formula 1 pit-stop design.
• In healthcare, the 'Resuscitation Trolley' model demonstrably reduces time-to-intervention in cardiac arrest scenarios.
• The HALO Trolley replicates this proven 'Resuscitation Trolley' model for low-frequency high-risk procedures.

QRH SYSTEM DESIGN (HALO Booklet v2.6, April 2026):
• Draws on aviation checklist design principles (Gawande, The Checklist Manifesto, 2009).
• Draws on NHS Emergency Department Checklist programme methodology.
• Human factors design: Two-page system (Page A: Walkthrough/Preparation; Page B: Action/Flowchart).
• Reader allocation to reduce cognitive load for the operator during high-stress procedures.

GUIDELINES REFERENCED FOR QRH CONTENT VALIDATION (verified April 2026 v2.6):
• DAS Guidelines — Surgical Airway (eFONA), Difficult Airway Society 2025
• RCEM Clinical Standards — Thoracotomy (FPHC & RCEM Consensus 2018/2025)
• Resuscitation Council UK 2021 — Resuscitative Hysterotomy (Perimortem C-Section)
• RCOG Green-top 20b — Emergency Breech Delivery
• BTS & RCEM Guidelines 2025 — Chest Drain (Open)
• ESC Guidelines 2015/2025 — Pericardiocentesis & External Pacing
• AAGBI Safety Guideline 2023 — LAST (Lipid Rescue)
• LSEBN Guidelines 2025 — Escharotomy (Burns)
• AAGBI Safe Vascular Access 2016 — Subclavian Central Line
• RCOphth Guidelines — Lateral Canthotomy
• RCEM Guidelines — Severe Max-Fax Bleed
checklist.results_text:
BASELINE PHASE RESULTS (as of April 2026):

Stopwatch Audit Baseline Data (N=13, Oct 2025 – Apr 2026):
• Baseline Median 'Time to Kit': 245 seconds
• High variation observed — range approximately 91s (centralized control) to >480s (dispersed HALO kit)
• Pilot comparison (N=3): Central Line Trolley (centralised) = 91s vs Thoracotomy kit (dispersed) = >300s

Pre-intervention Staff Confidence Survey:
• 9% of staff reported 'confident to assist/perform with guidance'

PDSA 1 STATUS (April 2026):
HALO Booklet v2.6 distributed to consultant and middle grade body. Feedback collection in progress.

PDSA 2 STATUS (Pending):
Trolley frame quote received (Charlotte Vineham, March 2026). Build pending. Nursing tag system agreed with Sarah Hart (26 April 2026).

NOTE: Post-intervention data collection will commence upon trolley deployment. This section will be updated with post-PDSA 2 Stopwatch Audit timings and post-intervention confidence survey results.
checklist.results_analysis:
ANALYSIS PLAN (to be completed post-intervention):

Run Chart analysis will use IHI rules to identify non-random variation:
• Rule 1 (Shift): 6+ consecutive points all above or below the baseline median of 245s
• Rule 2 (Trend): 5+ consecutive points all consistently increasing or decreasing
• Post-intervention: looking for a sustained downward shift below 245s median

For procedural knowledge: Wilcoxon signed-rank test (pre vs post QRH deployment scores)

CURRENT BASELINE STATUS:
Baseline median of 245s established (N=13). Run chart displays high variation consistent with a system dependent on memory and unfamiliar cupboard locations. No special cause variation detected in baseline phase — confirming an unpredictable, unstable process.
checklist.learning_points:
KEY LEARNING POINTS (updated as project progresses):

1. DUAL-ARM STRATEGY — PDSA cycles were deliberately reordered to start with the cognitive aid booklet (PDSA 1) before introducing the physical trolley (PDSA 2). This builds familiarity with kit requirements before centralising equipment, ensuring staff know what to look for before the trolley exists.

2. MOVING FROM 'WORK AS IMAGINED' TO 'WORK AS DONE' — I assumed staff knew where HALO kit was stored. The baseline Stopwatch Audit revealed a significant safety gap that was invisible until measured. Quantitative evidence was essential for stakeholder buy-in.

3. STAKEHOLDER NAVIGATION REQUIRES TAILORED COMMUNICATION:
• Matron/Ops Manager: Framed as 'Risk Reduction' and cost-neutral consolidation — not new spend
• Consultant Leads: Data-driven conversations with pilot timing evidence and patient safety framing
• Senior Sister: Focused on operational simplicity — agreed tag system that adds minimal workload

4. DELEGATION WITH CLEAR ACCOUNTABILITY:
• Dr Maida (SHO) — data collection, with clear output: '10 timings by Friday'
• Jemma (EDP) — nursing dissemination, with defined scope
• Portfolio evidence explicitly offered to junior team members as incentive

5. SMALL PDSA CYCLES — PDSA 3 (Kit Update for Thoracotomy/Hysterotomy trays) was added iteratively after discovering theatre trays are unwieldy for emergency use — demonstrating genuine iterative QI methodology.

LIMITATIONS:
• Single-site design — generalisability limited, but underlying principle is transferable
• Small sample (N=13) — mitigated by using Run Chart methodology (IHI-validated for small N)
• Hawthorne effect — mitigated by scenario-based timing starting at 'Scenario Declaration'
checklist.sustainability:
SUSTAINABILITY PLAN — Transition from 'Project' to 'Business as Usual':

STRUCTURAL:
• Tag system embedded: Team that uses the kit replaces drawer contents immediately post-case
• Twice-daily visual tag integrity check at handover (Senior Sister Sarah Hart as lead)
• Stock discrepancies reported via Datix — formally embedded in governance
• Trolley maintenance added to standard departmental checking schedule

CLINICAL CREDIBILITY:
• QRH guides peer-reviewed by consultant body before deployment (Mr Nadeem Zia, Dr Safdar Iman)
• Annual Q4 review of QRH content against updated national guidelines

MONITORING — QUARTERLY AUDIT SCHEDULE (2026/27):
• Q1: Compliance Check — audit the Daily Checklist for completion
• Q2: Staff Survey — re-run confidence survey for new rotation of Junior Doctors
• Q3: Spot Check — random Stopwatch Audit (N=5) to ensure 'Time to Kit' remains low
• Q4: Annual Review — update HALO QRH Guides to match any changes in national guidelines

IMPLEMENTATION PLAYBOOK:
1. MATRON PITCH — Frame as 'Risk Reduction' not 'New Equipment':
   'While there is a small initial setup cost for the trolley frame, this project generates long-term savings. Centralising stock reduces waste costs significantly while bridging a critical patient safety gap.'

2. TAG SYSTEM (Nursing Lead: Sarah Hart, Senior Sister):
   Step 1 — Clinical Staff: Break tag and use kit. Team that uses the kit replaces drawer contents immediately post-case.
   Step 2 — Integrity Check: Twice daily at handover, visual tag check only (no item count needed if tag intact).
   Step 3 — If Broken: Shift Coordinator alerts Stores Team (Khan Zaman) if items missing.

3. JUNIOR LEADERSHIP PROTOCOL (SHO Lead: Dr Maida):
   Define the output: 'I need 10 timings by Friday.'
   Weekly Friday review of data sheets together.
   Portfolio QIP participation assessment offered upon completion.
checklist.next_pdp:
PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PLANS FOLLOWING THIS PROJECT:

1. DISSEMINATION:
   Present findings at the Regional RCEM meeting and submit for publication in EMJ or RCEM Learning platform. The HALO Trolley and QRH system methodology is potentially transferable to other departments and Trusts — the underlying principle generalises even if specific timings do not.

2. SYSTEMATIC REVIEW:
   Conduct a formal systematic review of equipment centralisation interventions in emergency medicine settings, building a stronger evidence base for this model and contributing to the national evidence base.

3. LEADERSHIP DEVELOPMENT:
   This project has developed negotiation and stakeholder engagement skills across multiple professional groups (consultants, nursing, operations, pharmacy). I aim to apply these skills to a cross-specialty QIP at the ED/ITU interface in my next training post.

4. RCEM PORTFOLIO:
   Use this project to fulfil the FRCEM QIP portfolio domain requirement and complete QIAT sign-off with Educational Supervisor. Provide portfolio evidence for Dr Maida (SHO) and Jemma (EDP) as junior team members.

5. QRH PROGRAMME:
   Explore whether the WMEBEM HALO QRH Booklet (v2.6) can be adopted more widely across West Midlands EDs, potentially as a regional resource.

secondary: Staff knowledge of HALO procedure kit requirements
Availability of cognitive aids at point-of-care
Clear restocking and tag integrity protocol
Staff confidence in performing/assisting HALO procedures
changes: PDSA 1: HALO QRH Booklet — Peer-reviewed cognitive aid for 12 HALO procedures
PDSA 2: HALO Trolley — Centralise 8 core HALO procedure kits in dedicated Resus trolley
PDSA 3: Kit Update — Streamline Thoracotomy & Hysterotomy theatre trays for ED use
Tag system for stock integrity checks (agreed with Senior Sister Sarah Hart, 26 Apr 2026)
Pre/Post Knowledge Assessment to measure QRH educational impact
Stopwatch Audit (In-situ sim) for objective 'Time to Kit' measurement

causes.text: Skill decay between rare HALO events
Reliance on institutional memory for kit locations
Kit dispersed across multiple cupboards and stores
Theatre trays unwieldy for emergency use
No dedicated HALO procedure storage area
QRH guides not immediately accessible at point-of-care
Cognitive load during high-stress HALO events
Department layout creates long retrieval distances
Kit stored in locked cupboards requiring keys
No dedicated budget line for HALO equipment
LOCSSIPs compliance not previously audited for HALO procedures
No previous audit of Time to Kit for HALO procedures

process: Kit Not Found → Search Secondary Location
Set Up Equipment Under Time Pressure

pdsa.title: PDSA Cycle 1: Cognitive Infrastructure — HALO Booklet (QRH)
pdsa.plan:
PLAN: Draft the HALO QRH Booklet (v2.6) covering 12 HALO procedures verified against DAS, RCEM, FPHC, Resuscitation Council UK, RCOG, BTS, ESC, AAGBI, LSEBN and RCOphth guidelines. Distribute to consultant and middle grade body for peer review on clinical accuracy and layout.

PREDICTION: Exposure to structured cognitive aids will increase staff confidence scores and improve familiarity with kit requirements before the physical trolley is introduced. Creating familiarity with the 'what' before the 'where' is a deliberate sequencing decision.
pdsa.do: DO (April 2026): Booklet v2.6 distributed via email and physical copies placed in the ED staff room. Consultant and middle grade body invited to provide written feedback on clinical accuracy, drug doses, and procedural steps. Distribution completed April 2026 — feedback collection in progress.
pdsa.study:
STUDY (In progress — feedback being collated):
Plan: Collate feedback on clinical accuracy, completeness, and layout. Identify any errors, omissions, or outdated references requiring correction before attaching guides to the trolley in Cycle 2.

Pre-intervention knowledge assessment to be administered before finalising and deploying booklet.
pdsa.act:
ACT (Pending feedback collation):
Expected action: Finalise booklet content and format based on consultant feedback. Correct any clinical errors identified. Print final version for attachment to HALO Trolley in Cycle 2.

If significant errors found: Revise and recirculate for second peer review. If minor corrections only: Incorporate and finalise for Cycle 2 deployment.

pdsa.title: PDSA Cycle 2: Physical Infrastructure — HALO Trolley
pdsa.plan:
PLAN: Use the confirmed trolley frame quote (Charlotte Vineham, March 2026) to procure and build a dedicated HALO Trolley. Stock with 8 core HALO procedure kits. Implement the agreed tag system with Senior Sister Sarah Hart (26 April 2026). Deploy in the Resus bay.

PREDICTION: Centralising all HALO kit into one clearly labelled trolley will reduce mean 'Time to Kit' by ≥50% from the baseline median of 245 seconds. We predict this will be the highest-impact single intervention.
pdsa.do:
DO (Planned — pending trolley frame procurement completion):
Trolley frame to be ordered via Charlotte Vineham (Operations Manager) and Khan Zaman (Stores Lead).
Kit to be loaded using the Master Stock List (Appendix 5 of QIP paperwork).
Tag system implemented as agreed with Sarah Hart.
Trolley sited in Resus bay at agreed location.
Dr Maida (SHO) briefed to collect post-intervention Stopwatch Audit timings.
pdsa.study:
STUDY (Planned):
Dr Maida (SHO) to collect minimum 10 post-intervention Stopwatch Audit timings using same scenario-based protocol.
Compare against baseline N=13 dataset (median 245s).
Apply IHI Run Chart rules — looking specifically for Rule 1 (Shift: 6+ consecutive points below baseline median of 245s) to confirm sustained improvement.
Collect post-intervention staff confidence survey for comparison against baseline (9%).
pdsa.act:
ACT (Planned):
If improvement confirmed: ADOPT and report at ED Governance meeting. Begin quarterly monitoring schedule.
Adjust drawer dividers if any specific kit is difficult to extract quickly.
If improvement insufficient: Review specific procedures causing ongoing delays and target for PDSA 3 adaptation.

pdsa.title: PDSA Cycle 3: Kit Update — Thoracotomy & Hysterotomy Trays
pdsa.plan:
PLAN: Review the current standard theatre procedure trays for Resuscitative Thoracotomy and Resuscitative Hysterotomy, which are large, heavy, and unwieldy for emergency ED use. These trays were designed for theatre settings and contain items that are redundant in the ED emergency context.

PREDICTION: Streamlining tray contents will further reduce 'Time to Kit' for these specific high-stakes procedures and reduce cognitive clutter and confusion under maximum stress conditions.
pdsa.do:
DO (Planned):
Audit the current Thoracotomy and Hysterotomy tray contents against RCEM recommendations and WMEBEM QRH Booklet v2.6 equipment lists.
Identify redundant items not required for ED emergency use (theatre-specific items).
Document tray weight, setup time, and accessibility compared to streamlined alternatives.
pdsa.study:
STUDY (Planned):
Document all redundant items identified.
Compare Stopwatch Audit times for Thoracotomy and Hysterotomy specifically before and after tray modification.
Review with Nadeem Zia (Trauma Lead) and Charlotte Vineham (Procurement) for clinical and cost sign-off.
pdsa.act:
ACT (Planned):
Create a formal proposal for bespoke, streamlined ED procedural trays for Resuscitative Thoracotomy and Hysterotomy.
Present to Stores Team (Khan Zaman) and Procurement (Charlotte Vineham).
If approved: Commission bespoke trays. If cost-prohibitive: Identify which theatre tray items can be pre-removed to create a 'lite' ED version.

leadershipLogs.note: PROJECT INITIATED — Pilot Scoping Audit conducted (N=3). Centralised stock (Central Line Trolley): 91s vs dispersed HALO stock (Thoracotomy kit): >300s. Proof of concept confirmed. Critical safety gap identified and quantified. Decision made to proceed with dual-arm QIP (Cognitive + Physical infrastructure).

Dr Maida (SHO) allocated as Junior Doctor Lead for Stopwatch Audit data collection. Clear expectations set: '10 timings by Friday each week.' Weekly Friday data review meetings scheduled. Portfolio QIP participation assessment offered on completion.

Mr Nadeem Zia (Consultant, Trauma Lead) — Clinical sign-off meeting. Approved trolley contents and QRH guide content. Recommended consultant peer review of all guides before deployment. Key stakeholder secured for clinical credibility.
Jemma (EDP) allocated as Nursing Dissemination Lead. Responsible for coordinating kit review and supporting the checking/restocking process design.

Charlotte Vineham (Operations Manager) — TROLLEY QUOTE RECEIVED. Charlotte formally sent the trolley frame quote, removing the key operational and financial barrier to physical implementation. Cleared to proceed with build plans.
Dr Safdar Iman (Consultant, Resus Lead) — CLINICAL ENDORSEMENT CONFIRMED. Authorised re-allocation of stock from existing Resus cupboards to the HALO Trolley. Key approval for stock movement secured.

PDSA 1 LAUNCHED — HALO Booklet v2.6 distributed to consultant and middle grade body for peer review. Physical copies placed in the ED staff room. Email distribution completed. Feedback requested on clinical accuracy, drug doses, and layout before finalising for trolley deployment.

Sarah Hart (Senior Sister) — NURSING TAG SYSTEM & RESTOCK RULE AGREED. Met with Sarah Hart to finalise operational trolley plan. AGREED: (1) Drawers tagged with visual seals for quick integrity check. (2) Team that uses the kit is responsible for replacing drawer contents immediately post-case. (3) Twice-daily visual tag check at handover. (4) Broken tag triggers alert to Stores Team (Khan Zaman) if items missing.

gantt.name: PDSA 1: HALO Booklet — Draft, Peer Review & Feedback

surveys.title: Pre-Intervention Staff Confidence Survey — HALO Procedures
questions: How confident are you to ASSIST with HALO procedures? (1=Not at all, 5=Fully confident)
How confident are you to PERFORM HALO procedures with senior guidance? (1=Not at all, 5=Fully confident)
Do you know where ALL the kit is for HALO procedures? (1=No idea, 5=Always know)
Would a dedicated HALO kit trolley improve your ability to respond? (1=No, 5=Definitely yes)
Would bedside QRH guides for HALO procedures be useful? (1=No, 5=Definitely yes)
Which HALO procedures are you LEAST confident about?
surveys.summary: Pre-intervention baseline: 9% of staff reported being 'confident to assist/perform with guidance' for HALO procedures during initial scoping questionnaire. This survey should be re-administered after PDSA 1 (booklet deployment) and after PDSA 2 (trolley deployment) to track improvement against the 90% target.
